sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - Clicking (53, 10) moved the magenta square from (56-58, 5-7) to (52-54, 9-11) - Mechanic confirmed: clicking on a gray dot moves the nearest magenta square to that position - The old position becomes a gray dot - I can move the magenta square to any gray dot position by clicking on it - Current magenta positions: (4-6, 30-32) and (52-54, 9-11) - Need to figure out the goal - maybe move magenta square to the blue circle
- Plan: Try clicking on the gray dot closest to the blue circle (17, 46) to see if the magenta square can jump directly there.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 12 of about 22 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Clicking (17, 46) had NO effect on the magenta squares or gray dots. The magenta positions and gray dots are exactly the same before and after. But the result says `gameplay_changed=True`. This is confusing.
Wait, let me look more carefully. The magenta positions are the same, and the gray dots are the same. But `gameplay_changed=True`. Maybe something else changed that I didn't check?
Let me think about what could have changed. The blue circle? The top magenta square? The charcoal bar?
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the mechanic. Earlier, clicking on gray dots near the magenta square moved it. But clicking on (17, 46) which is far from the magenta square didn't move it.
So the mechanic might be: clicking on a gray dot moves the NEAREST magenta square to that position. But (17, 46) is far from both magenta squares, so maybe it doesn't work?
Or maybe the mechanic is: clicking on a gray dot moves the magenta square to that position ONLY IF the gray dot is adjacent to the magenta square's current position along the path.
Let me reconsider the earlier moves:
1. Clicking (55, 8) moved magenta from (52-54, 9-11) to (54-56, 7-9) - (55, 8) is between these two positions on the path
2. Clicking (57, 6) moved magenta from (54-56, 7-9) to (56-58, 5-7) - (57, 6) is between these two positions
3. Clicking (53, 10) moved magenta from (56-58, 5-7) to (52-54, 9-11) - (53, 10) is between these two positions
So the mechanic is: clicking on a gray dot that is ADJACENT to the magenta square's current position along the path moves the magenta square to the other side of that gray dot.
Wait, that doesn't quite work either. Let me think more carefully.
The path positions are: ..., (51, 12), (53, 10), (55, 8), (57, 6), ...
The magenta square is a 3x3 object, so its center is at the middle row and column.
When the magenta square was at (52-54, 9-11), its center was at (53, 10).
When I clicked (55, 8), the magenta square moved to (54-56, 7-9), center at (55, 8).
So clicking on (55, 8) moved the magenta square from center (53, 10) to center (55, 8).
Then clicking on (57, 6) moved it from center (55, 8) to center (57, 6).
Then clicking on (53, 10) moved it from center (57, 6) to center (53, 10).
So the mechanic is: clicking on a gray dot moves the magenta square to that gray dot's position, but ONLY if the gray dot is adjacent to the current position along the path.
When I clicked (17, 46), which is far from the current position (53, 10), nothing happened because it's not adjacent.
So to move the magenta square along the path, I need to click on gray dots that are adjacent to its current position.
The current magenta square center is at (53, 10). The adjacent gray dots are (51, 12) and (55, 8).
If I want to move it towards the blue circle, I should click on (51, 12), then (49, 14), etc.
But this would take many actions. Let me think about whether there's a faster way.
Actually, wait. Let me reconsider the goal. Maybe the goal is NOT to move the magenta square to the blue circle. Maybe the goal is something else.
Let me think about what the game could be:
1. The white cross was a "target" that, when clicked, moved the magenta square there.
2. The gray dots form a path, and clicking on adjacent gray dots moves the magenta square along the path.
3. The blue circle might be the final destination.
But moving the magenta square step by step along the path would take many actions, which is inefficient.
Alternatively, maybe the goal is to move the magenta square to a specific position on the path, and I need to figure out which position.
Let me try a different approach. Let me click on (51, 12) to move the magenta square one step towards the blue circle, and see what happens.
Actually, I realize I should also consider the top magenta square at (4-6, 30-32). Maybe I need to move that one too.
Let me think about the path more carefully. The path goes from (17, 46) near the blue circle to (57, 6) at the bottom-left. The top magenta square at (4-6, 30-32) is NOT on this path.
Wait, but (31, 32) is a gray dot position! And the top magenta square is at (4-6, 30-32), which has center (5, 31). The gray dot at (31, 32) is on the path.
Hmm, the top magenta square's center is (5, 31), which is NOT on the path. The path goes through (31, 32), which is much lower.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the top magenta square is not related to the path at all. Or maybe there's a different path for it.
Actually, let me look at the initial state again. The initial state had:
- Magenta square at (58-60, 3-5) - this is near the bottom of the path
- Magenta square at (4-6, 30-32) - this is at the top-center
- White cross at (52-54, 9-11) - this is on the path
- Blue circle at (11-19, 44-52)
- Gray dots from (17, 46) to (57, 6)
The white cross was clicked, and the magenta square at (58-60, 3-5) moved to (52-54, 9-11). This makes sense because the white cross was a target.
Now, the magenta square is at (52-54, 9-11), and I need to figure out what to do next.
Let me try clicking on (51, 12) to move the magenta square one step towards the blue circle.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model:
- Clicking (17, 46) had NO effect - magenta squares and gray dots unchanged
- Mechanic refined: clicking a gray dot only moves the magenta square if the dot is ADJACENT to its current position along the path
- Current magenta center is at (53, 10); adjacent gray dots are (51, 12) and (55, 8)
- To move towards the blue circle, need to click (51, 12), then (49, 14), etc.
- This step-by-step movement is slow but seems to be the only way
- Need to figure out the actual goal - maybe move magenta square to blue circle
Plan: Click on (51, 12) to move the magenta square one step towards the blue circle, then continue along the path.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Click on (51, 12) to move magenta towards blue circle
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 51, 'col': 12}])
print(f"Click (51,12): board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}, no_impact={last_action_result.get('no_impact')}")

# Check magenta positions
before = last_transition.before_frame
after = last_transition.after_frame

before_p = []
for n in before.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p':
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min([p[0] for p in b]), max([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b]), max([p[1] for p in b]))
        before_p.append(r)

after_p = []
for n in after.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p':
        b = n['boundary']
        r = (min([p[0] for p in b]), max([p[0] for p in b]), min([p[1] for p in b]), max([p[1] for p in b]))
        after_p.append(r)

print(f"Magenta before: {before_p}")
print(f"Magenta after: {after_p}")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
